sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “You Lifted Me Out of the Depths”

heb.gloss: you lifted me / you drew me up
heb.paragraph: "I will exalt you, LORD, for you lifted me out (dillîtānî) of the depths" (v.1). The verb is from drawing water from a well — David was deep in the pit and God pulled him up. For a dedication of the temple (superscription). God healed David, brought him up from the realm of the dead (v.3). "Weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning" (v.5).

panels.ctx: A thanksgiving for healing. David was near death (v.3), cried for help (v.2), and God healed him. The psalm’s most famous verse: "weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning" (v.5). The night of suffering has a time limit; morning always comes.

cross.note: "For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with deep compassion I will bring you back" — the same temporary-suffering theology.
"Our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory" — Paul’s version of morning joy.

notes.note: "LORD my God, I called to you for help, and you healed me. You brought me up from the realm of the dead." From death’s edge to life. The healing is both physical and theological.
"Weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning." The psalm’s thesis: suffering is temporary; joy is coming. Night has an expiration date.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

sections.header: Verses 8–12 — “You Turned My Wailing into Dancing”

heb.paragraph: "You turned my wailing into dancing (māḥōl); you removed my sackcloth and clothed me with joy" (v.11). The most dramatic reversal image in the Psalter: wailing → dancing, sackcloth → joy. God does not merely stop suffering; he transforms it into celebration. The psalm closes: "LORD my God, I will praise you forever" (v.12).

panels.ctx: David cried for mercy (v.8), argued against his death (vv.9–10: "what is gained by my destruction? Will the dust praise you?"), and God responded by transforming everything: wailing → dancing, sackcloth → joy-clothing (v.11). The psalm closes with the vow: praise forever (v.12).

cross.note: "I will turn their mourning into gladness" — the same reversal promise applied nationally.
"He will wipe every tear from their eyes" — the eschatological morning.

notes.note: "What is gained if I am silenced? Will the dust praise you? Will it proclaim your faithfulness?" David argues: keep me alive because dead people can’t worship. The argument from praise.
"You turned my wailing into dancing; you clothed me with joy, that my heart may sing your praises and not be silent." The transformation is total: sorrow’s wardrobe replaced with joy’s garments.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: "What is gained if I am silenced? Will the dust praise you? Will it proclaim your faithfulness?" David argues: keep me alive because dead people can’t worship…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "LORD my God, I called to you for help, and you healed me…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWhen I felt secure, I said, “I will never be shaken.”ESVAs for me, I said in my prosperity, \u201cI shall never be moved.\u201dNIVYou, Lord, brought me up from the realm of the dead; you spared me from going down to the pit.ESVO LORD, you have brought up my soul from Sheol; you restored me to life from among those who go down to the pit.

rec.text: ""LORD my God, I called to you for help, and you healed me. You brought me up from the realm of the dead." From death’s edge to life. The healing is both physical and theological."

rows.label: The Dance — 30:8–12
rows.text: "You turned my wailing into dancing." Sackcloth → joy. "I will praise you forever."

hebtext.gloss: you lifted me / you drew me up
hebtext.note: "I will exalt you, LORD, for you lifted me out (dillîtānî) of the depths" (v.1). The verb is from drawing water from a well — David was deep in the pit and God pulled him up. For a dedication of the temple (superscription). God healed David, brought him up from the realm of the dead (v.3). "Weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning" (v.5).

"You turned my wailing into dancing (māḥōl); you removed my sackcloth and clothed me with joy" (v.11). The most dramatic reversal image in the Psalter: wailing → dancing, sackcloth → joy. God does not merely stop suffering; he transforms it into celebration. The psalm closes: "LORD my God, I will praise you forever" (v.12).

thread.text: "For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with deep compassion I will bring you back" — the same temporary-suffering theology.
"Our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory" — Paul’s version of morning joy.
"I will turn their mourning into gladness" — the same reversal promise applied nationally.
"He will wipe every tear from their eyes" — the eschatological morning.

themes.note: Mercy and Worship both peak: weeping → morning, wailing → dancing. The psalm IS transformation theology.
